coredata.dc:title: Deep desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel fuel over Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 under ultraviolet radiation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lei
Zuo, Ning
Sun, Meng
Ma, Yingying
Mominou, Nchare
Jiang, Weijie
Li, Shuzhen
Jing, Chunyu
coredata.dc:description: Designing an efficient and environmentally-friendly catalyst for desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel to substitute for widely used catalysts is essential. We have prepared Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 by doping N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr in TiO2. The results suggested that turnover frequencies were much higher than those of the others, which indicated that the prepared catalyst had an excellent activity of desulfurization and denitrogenation under ultraviolet radiation. Turnover frequencies of benzothiophene, dibenzothiophene, and 4, 6-dimethyldibenzothiophene were 62.8, 78.4, and 92.4, and turnover frequencies of quinolone, indole, and carbazole were 64.5, 88.4, and 86.5, respectively. The ratios of desulfurization and denitrogenation reached 99.8% and 95.5%. The results of PL, UV–vis, and EPR showed the doping of N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr could extend the range of photo-absorption and improve the hole-electron separation of TiO2. This might be because of the N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr increasing oxygen vacancies and defects on the catalytic surface, forming lower gap energy and rapidly transferring and reserving electrons under ultraviolet radiation. Also, the photocatalytic mechanism of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was proposed in the present work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Desulfurization and denitrogenation
Clean fuel
Co-doping
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work carried out at Shanghai Institute of Technology was sponsored by Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 19ZR1454900 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectra of samples (Raman (a), PL (b), Py-FTIR(c), UV-Vis(d), and EPR(e)).
Influence of metal ratios and metal loading on desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel.
Influence of reaction time on desulfurization (a) and denitrogenation (b) of diesel.
Influence of reaction temperature on desulfurization (a) and denitrogenation (b) of diesel
Influence of space velocity (a) and oxidant dosage (b) on desulfurization (a) and denitrogenation (b) of diesel.
Investigation of catalyst stability on desulfurization (a) and denitrogenation (b) of diesel (Time 50 min, Temperature 373K,), and TG curves of samples (c).

head.ce:title: Deep desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel fuel over Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 under ultraviolet radiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 201418, PR China (C. Jing).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: This author contributed equally to this work and should be considered the co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing an efficient and environmentally-friendly catalyst for desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel to substitute for widely used catalysts is essential. We have prepared Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 by doping N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr in TiO2. The results suggested that turnover frequencies were much higher than those of the others, which indicated that the prepared catalyst had an excellent activity of desulfurization and denitrogenation under ultraviolet radiation. Turnover frequencies of benzothiophene, dibenzothiophene, and 4, 6-dimethyldibenzothiophene were 62.8, 78.4, and 92.4, and turnover frequencies of quinolone, indole, and carbazole were 64.5, 88.4, and 86.5, respectively. The ratios of desulfurization and denitrogenation reached 99.8% and 95.5%. The results of PL, UV–vis, and EPR showed the doping of N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr could extend the range of photo-absorption and improve the hole-electron separation of TiO2. This might be because of the N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr increasing oxygen vacancies and defects on the catalytic surface, forming lower gap energy and rapidly transferring and reserving electrons under ultraviolet radiation. Also, the photocatalytic mechanism of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was proposed in the present work.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few years, sulfur and nitrogen oxides produced by burning fuels are one of the causes of air pollution, specially hazy weather and acid rain [1,2]. Therefore, strict regulations on the permissible sulfur content in fuel have been performed, which presented a challenge for the removal of S-compounds in fuel. The competitive adsorption of sulfides and nitrides on the active center in the hydrodesulfurization (HDS) could lead to a decrease in removal ratios of the sulfur compounds and catalyst poisoning. Besides, the reaction between basic nitrogen and acid sites led to inhibition effects on HDS. Laredo et al. [3] considered that nitrides had a restrained effect on the removal of sulfides at a concentration of 5 μg/g. Also, there was a negative effect of pyrrolic compounds on fuel stability due to the pyrrolic autoxidation. Therefore, desulfurization with the simultaneous denitrogenation of diesel fuel is necessary.

In the past literature, many researchers have described desulfurization and denitrogenation of transport fuel [4]. Alternative methods of HDS have been studied, including selective adsorption, extraction, oxidation, and their combination. Mohammadian et al. [5–7] studied the process of desulfurization and denitrogenation of fuel oil by adsorption with mesostructure adsorbent and its modified one by metallic oxide. Rezvani et al. [8,9] have synthesized novel organic–inorganic hybrid materials with superior performances for oxidation desulfurization (ODS) of fuel oil. Li et al. [10] described oxidative desulfurization and denitrogenation of fuel using metal–organic framework-based/-derived catalysts under oxygen as an oxidant. Some studies have shown that the ODS process can effectively oxidize sulfur compounds to sulfones in diesel oil under the action of catalyst [11–13]. However, removing the oxides of sulfides and nitrides of fuel needed polar solvents because of the low water solubility of the oxidized products, which reduced the yield of refined diesel fuel. Therefore, selective oxidation was combined with in-situ hydrogenation to improve the removal efficiency of sulfides and nitrides [14,15], and it is carried out at low pressure and temperature compared with the other desulfurization and denitrogenation. Nitrides and sulfides were oxidized to N-oxides and sulfones, and then hydrogenated to hydrocarbon compounds [16].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can effectively reduce the electrical resistance between the interfaces of two semiconductors, have an extraordinary talent to hinder the recombination of the charge carriers, improve light absorption, and open up a new way for efficiently using the full spectrum. Also, CQDs show an excellent electron reservoir and transfer property because of their conjugated π structure. Therefore, many kinds of CQDs and TiO2 have been combined to improve the photo activity[17–19]. Our research team investigated the removal of thiophene sulfides in diesel using TiO2 composites and achieved encouraging results. Further research is needed to explore the potential of CQDs based on TiO2 for the simultaneous removal of sulfides and nitrides in diesel.

The f orbital of rare earth(RE)elements can promote the surface adsorption of TiO2, the complexation of sulfide, and the separation of photogenerated carriers. Ramya et al.’s [20] studies showed that Pr doped TiO2 could cause the redshift of the light absorption edge of the visible region and enhance its photocatalytic performance. However, the conductivity of the rare earth metal oxide was poor and it is composited with CQDs or carbon to improve its dispersibility and conductivity. Iridium (Ir) is a transition metal with high stability and easy doping. Besides, Ir displayed an exceptional solid phase characteristic, which was contributed to exposing catalytic sites preferentially. Therefore, it was meaningful to explore the influence of Ir and Pr co-doping on the modification of TiO2.

At present, there are few studies on modifying composites of CQDs and TiO2 by co-doping of RE, transition, and N elements for the desulfurization with simultaneous denitrogenation of diesel fuel. Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 by loading rare earth and transition elements on the N-CQDs-TiO2 was prepared, and Ti3+ species and oxygen vacancies, which were regulated by the proportion of Ir/Pr, were successfully introduced into TiO2 by a simple method. Characterization results showed the doping of Ir, Pr, N, and CQDs could improve surface defects of mesoporous titania. Defective mesoporous titania showed desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios were higher than those of other photocatalysts because of inhibiting the recombination of photo-excited charge carriers. Also, the possible mechanism of the removal of sulfides and nitrides in diesel fuel over the defective photocatalyst was proposed.

All chemicals were of analytical reagents and used without further purification. Iridium nitrate, praseodymium nitrate, glucose, decane, benzothiophene (BT), dibenzothiophene (DBT), 4, 6-dimethyldibenzothiophene (4, 6-DMDBT), quinolone, indole, carbazole, 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1,13- tridecanediamine, titanium isopropoxide, sodium hydroxide, and hydrogen peroxide were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Co. BT (0.5872 g), DBT (0.5872 g), 4,6-DMDBT (0.2936 g), quinolone (0.0441 g), indole (0.0440 g), and carbazole (0.0587 g) were dissolved in decane in volumetric flask (2000 mL) to form the decane solution of sulfides (1000 μg/g) and nitrides(100 μg/g), which was used as the model of diesel fuel.

CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal carbonization method. In a typical synthesis, 1 g of glucose was mixed with 200 mL distilled water under stirring, which was adjusted to pH 9.5 by sodium hydroxide solution, under in ultrasonic for 5 min. Then the solution was put into the reactor and heated at 573 K for 11 h. After cooling, the solution was filtered with the membrane (0.22 μm) to remove biomass-based, large aggregates, and dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MD55, 36 mm) to get the solution of CQDs for further experiments.

Ir/Pr-N-CQDS-TiO2 was prepared by the hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, a mixture of 10% solution of 4, 7, 10-trioxa-1, 13-tridecanediamine (20 μL) and titanium isopropoxide solution (100 mL) was added to the mixture of 100 mL CQDs solution, 100 mL iridium nitrate (0.05 mol/L), and 100 mL praseodymium nitrate (0.05 mol/L) under vigorous stirring. During the preparation, the pH was adjusted to 7 by changing the flows of reagents. The precipitate was centrifugated and washed with deionized water until the washout conductivity was similar to distilled water. Finally, the resulting samples were centrifugated, washed twice with deionized water, and then dried at 343 K for 26 h. The precipitate was calcined under H2/argon at 279 K•min−1 up to 650 K for 2.5 h to get the Ir/Pr-N-CQDS-TiO2 (1:1). Based on the different ratios of Ir and Pr, the obtained composites were denoted as Ir/Pr-N-CQDS- TiO2 (1:0.5), Ir/Pr-N-CQDS-TiO2 (1:0.8), and Ir/Pr-N-CQDS-TiO2 (1:1.5). The N-CQDs- TiO2 sample was prepared in a similar process without iridium nitrate and praseodymium nitrate.

Turnover Frequency (TOF), desulfurization ratios, and denitrogenation ratios is used to explain the denitrogenation and desulfurization activity. TOF value, which reflects the intrinsic activity, refers to the moles of products produced by moles of surface metal atoms in a second. The calculation equations of TOF, denitrogenation ratios, and desulfurization ratios are as follows:
M e t a l
d i s p e r s i o n =
V 1
×
S 1
×
M 1
W 1
× 22414
× 100 %
T O F
(
s
- 1
)
=
F × Y
W 1
× W
M 1
× D
D e s u l f u r i z a t i o n
d e n i t r o g e n a t i o n
r a t i o s =
C 1
-
C 2
C 1
∗
100 %
where V1 (mL) represents the volume of the adsorbed CO, S1 represents the stoichiometry factor, M1 (g•mol−1) represents the metal atomic weight, W1 (g) represents the weight of the metal in the catalyst, F represents the moles of reactants, Y represents the yield of products, W represents the weight of reaction catalysts, and D represents the dispersion of metal (metal surface/metal total), C1 and C2 represent the mole content of sulfides or nitrides before and after the reaction.

The nitrogen desorption-adsorption isotherms of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) presented type IV with H3 shape hysteresis loop between the relative pressure of 0.7–0.95 (Fig. S1), showing the prepared catalyst with the mesoporous structure [21]. The specific surface area is 140.65 cm2/g. The pore-size distribution plot of Ir/Pr-N- CQDs-TiO2 is calculated by BJH method which is shown in the inset of Fig. S1. The pore size distribution ranges from 2 to 40 nm (inset of Fig. S1), and the mean pore diameter is 10.5 nm, which can reduce the diffusion resistance of the sulfide (nitride) macromolecular. Besides, bigger pores are conducive to the accessibility of desulfurization and denitrogenation active sites located in the internal surface [22]. From the BET specific surface areas and the pore size distribution plot of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2, the composites are successfully formed.

Morphology and microstructure are explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It can be seen from Fig. S2 that the nanospheres are irregular and well dispersed with an average size of about 20 nm (Fig. S2a), where active species (about 5 nm) are homogeneously spread on the Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (Fig. S2b). The EDS image for Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (Fig. S2c) shows the element content of C, N, O, Ti, Ir, and Pr is 0.363, 1.428, 46.10, 50.58, 0.755, and 0.766 (w)%. No other elements are involved in the Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 samples besides C, N, O, Ti, Ir, and Pr.

Fig. S3a shows the XRD curves of CQDs-TiO2, N-CQDs-TiO2, and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs- TiO2. It can be seen the diffraction peaks of the sample are both anatase and rutile. Peaks at 25.4°, 36.97°, 38.75°, 47.79°, 55.13°, and 62.11° are assigned to the anatase TiO2(PDF# 21–1272). The other peaks at 27.3°, 36.1°, 39.2°, 40.89°, 54.56°, 62.7°,and 69.12° are assigned to the rutile TiO2(PDF# 21–1276). The results show the original structure of samples with the doping of Ir, Pr, N, and CQDs is kept because the ionic radii of Pr3+ (1.01 A) and Ir4+ (0.63 A) are larger than Ti4+ (0.60 A) and thus cannot replace Ti4+ into the TiO2 lattice. Also, there were not the characteristic peaks of Ir, Pr, N, and CQDs, which is caused by the low content of Pr and Ir or the high dispersion of Pr and Ir on the TiO2 surface. The microcrystal size was calculated by Scherrer's formula. The average grain size of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was about 20 nm, which is consistent with the TEM results.

There are four peaks at 457.7, 458.6, 463.4, and 464.3 eV of Ti 2p (Fig. S4a). Peaks at 457.7 and 463.4 eV are assigned to Ti3+, which is attributed to the reduction of Ti4+ from Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2. And peaks of 458.6 and 464.3 eV are credited to Ti4+ [23]. The intensity of the peak is related to the defect content got from Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 [24]. Ir 4f XPS shows the characteristic peaks at 63.4 and 60.2 eV are assigned to the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of Ir0. And peaks at 65.1 and 61.9 eV are assigned to the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of Ir4+ [25], respectively (Fig. S4b). As shown in Fig. S4c, the Pr 3d XPS shows characteristic peaks of Pr 3d5/2 and Pr 3d3/2 at 954.2 and 933.2 eV, suggesting the Pr3+ existed on Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 [26].N 1 s of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (Fig. S4d) show three peaks at 398.2, 399.4, and 400.9 eV are credited to C-N = C, N-H and C-N bonds, which confirm that CQDs are modified by –NH2 group [27]. The C1s of the Ir/Pr- N-CQDs-TiO2 (Fig. S4e) reveals peaks of C = O, C-N, C-O, and C-C at 287.3, 285.7, 286, and 284.6 eV, which are the typical peaks of N-CQDs [28,29]. The O 1 s have peaks at 532.1and 530.0 eV, crediting to chemisorbed water (H2Oads) and lattice oxygen (M−O). An obvious peak that originates from C = O at 531.1 eV, revealing the existence of the CQDs [30–32]. It has been proved that water is absorbed on the oxygen vacancies and defects to consequently transform to ·OH, which interprets why surfaces can form more H2Oads [33,34]. According to XPS spectra, Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies are formed in Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2, which can narrow the bandgap and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

Raman spectroscopy is used for characterizing carbon structures. Two Raman bands at 1368 and 1597 cm−1 are detected (Fig. 1 a), which are assigned to the D-band and the G-band of carbon [35], showing the CQDs are successfully coupled with TiO2. The G-band provides information on sp2 bonded carbon, and the D-band is a feature for sp3 defects in carbon [36]. Based on the Raman spectra, the IG/ID of CQDs and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 can be calculated as 1.7624 and 1.7295.

PL spectra can analyze information such as the efficiency of charge carriers trapping, migration and transfer, defects, surface oxygen vacancies, and comprehend the life of hole-electron pairs [37]. An increase in PL intensity agrees with the fast recombination of hole-electron pairs, showing a decrease in photocatalytic activity. The PL intensity of the samples is changed by doping the different elements (Fig. 1b). We can see the intensity of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) is the lowest, and that of CQDs-TiO2 is the highest. The sequence of intensity is: Ir/Pr-N-CQDs- TiO2 (1:1) < Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2(1:1.5) < deactivated Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2(1:1) < Ir/Pr-N-CQDs- TiO2(1:0.8) < Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2(1:0.5) < N-CQDs-TiO2 < CQDs- TiO2. The results show suitable Ir/Pr ratios may improve the separation rate of electron and hole of TiO2, and the co-doping of N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr is more conducive to separating electron-hole pairs, resulting in the improvement of the catalytic activity under ultraviolet radiation.

Pyridine-FTIR is performed to get the Lewis and Brønsted acidity of the sample and the results are shown in Fig. 1c. Three peaks at 1623, 1576, and 1450 cm−1 are credited to Lewis acid, and the peak at 1492 cm−1 arises from the coexistence of Brønsted and Lewis acid.[38] Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2(1:1) has the highest Lewis and the largest B + L acid sites. Besides, the quantity of Brønsted acid increases as followed: Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) > Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1.5) > Ir/Pr-N- CQDs-TiO2 (1:0.8) > Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:0.5) > deactivated Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1). Compared with the fresh catalyst, the distribution of acid sites of the deactivated catalyst is changed according to the intensity of Lewis and Brønsted in Fig. 1c, which might be one of the reasons for a decrease in desulfurization and denitrogenation activities.

Improving light absorbance is the most important for photocatalytic activity. The absorption of ultraviolet radiation is improved, which shows that CQDs, N, Ir, and Pr play an important role in extending the light absorption region (Fig. 1d). A slight increase in the light absorption of N-CQDs-TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 could be remarked. Compared with N-CQDs-TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2, Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 shows a redshift, and the light absorption range is widened to 478 nm, because of forming more defects on the surface by modification of Ir, Pr, N, and CQDs. The results show the ultraviolet radiation region of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 is improved, and the use rate of ultraviolet radiation is increased.

The sample has a strong symmetrical peak at 2.002 and a weak peak at 1.987. The former are assigned to oxygen vacancies, and the latter belongs to the defect of Ti3 + (Fig. 1e). The results show the doping destroys the Ti-O bond, and some oxygen vacancies are produced on the surface of TiO2 after oxygen atoms are removed. Besides, the increase in the intensity of ESR peaks at 2.002 and 1.987 shows an increase in the concentration of oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ defects on the surface of TiO2 with the Ir:Pr ratios from 1:0.5 to 1:1. However, the intensity is decreased when the Ir:Pr ratio was 1:1.5. Compared with fresh Ir/Pr-N- CQDs-TiO2 (1:1), the peak intensity of the deactivated catalyst is reduced.
Table S1 shows the TOF value of sulfides and nitrides over different samples under ultraviolet radiation irradiation.

CQDs-TiO2, N-CQDs-TiO2, Ir/Pr-TiO2, and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 display better photocatalytic activity than TiO2, suggesting that N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr can promote the electron-hole separation and further improve the photocatalytic efficiency. This is consistent with the results of PL, UV–Vis, and EPR. Also, N-doping stabilizes the oxygen vacancy, which in turn improves the light absorption properties [39].

4, 6-DMDBT is the most difficult to be removed in the traditional HDS. However, the Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) shows the excellent activity of 4, 6-DMDBT. Mesoporous materials can provide pore channels for reactants leading to a decrease in the diffusion resistance, which is favorable for the removal of macromolecular sulfides such as 4, 6-DMDBT. Also, the desulfurization and denitrogenation active centers can scatter on the external and internal surface because of the pore structure, and provide accessibility for the active centers of sulfides and nitrides. Besides, the TiO2 offers a large surface area, making a high dispersion of the metal in Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2. Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 shows significant advantages not only in benefiting to disperse active sites, but also in easing the diffusion resistance and then improving the contact between reactants and activity centers. Also, suitable acidity plays an important role in the selectivity of desulfurization and denitrogenation. [40] The increase in Lewis and total acidity could be conducive to HDS. Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 with the largest Lewis acid and total acid displays the highest activity of HDS (Fig. 1c). Besides, an increase in Brønsted acid leads to higher proportions of direct hydrogenolysis desulfurization(DDS), which is considered as a more environmental and profitable technology compared with HYD for direct splitting C-S bonds using less hydrogen [41,42].

The metal ratios and loading of Ir and Pr on the N-CQDs-TiO2 can improve the desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios. An increase in desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios shows that higher metal ratios favor the reaction when metal ratios were increased from 0.5 to 1 (Fig. 2 a). However, there appeared a falling trend in desulfurization and a plateau in denitrogenation with a further increase in the metal ratios. Desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios reached a maximum of 98.8% and 95.5%, when the loading of Ir and Pr was 1.5%. Desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios increased from 55.4 to 98.8% and from 56.8 to 95.5%, respectively, with Ir and Pr loading from 0.1 to 1.5%. However, desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios reduced from 98.8 to 96.2% and from 95.5 to 94.1% with Ir and Pr loading from 1.5 to 2.5% (Fig. 2b). The experimental results and the characterization of PL, UV–Vis, and EPR indicate the high Ir and Pr content may decrease defects on the surface, resulting in a decrease in the photocatalytic activity [43].

The influence of reaction time on desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios of diesel over CQDs-TiO2, N-CQDs-TiO2, and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 under ultraviolet radiation is shown in Fig. 3 . Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 has the best photocatalytic performance because of extending the response range (Fig. 1d) and the high efficiency of electron-hole separation (Fig. 1b). Adsorption is performed without ultraviolet radiation for 20 min to determine the adsorption equilibrium. Desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios of diesel over CQDs-TiO2 are only 65.4% and 86.2% for 50 min. However, desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 increase to 98.8% and 95.5%, respectively. The corresponding concentrations of sulfide and nitrides in refined diesel are 2 and 0.2 μg/g, which are lower than those of CQDs-TiO2 (173 μg/g, 1.4 μg/g) and N-CQDs-TiO2 (54 μg/g, 1 μg/g). The reason for an increase in the photocatalytic activity of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 can be credited to form surface oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ defects, which can promote the separation and transport of photo-generated charge carriers [44].

The effect of temperature on desulfurization and denitrogenation of diesel is shown in Fig. 4 . Desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios increased when there was an increase in the reaction temperature. However, there is a decrease in desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios when the temperature was higher than 373 K. The possible reason is that when the temperature is higher than 373 K, we speculate that the desorption rate of hydrogen peroxide and methanol at the active sites is obviously accelerated, resulting in the decrease of active species for effective oxidation and hydrogenation, which reduces the catalytic performance resulting in the desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios. Therefore, 373 K is considered as the proper temperature of desulfurization with simultaneous denitrogenation.

When the ratio of Ir:Pr is 1:1, TOF value of sulfides and nitrides is the best (Table S1). TOF value increased and then decreased with the increase in ratios. The desulfurization and denitrogenation ratio increased with an increase in the ratio of Ir:Pr (Fig. 2a). The effect of Ir and Pr reduces the energy level of TiO2 and can effectively receive photogenerated electrons, resulting in the potential difference between the surface and the interior of the catalyst, preventing the recombination of photogenerated e- and h+, and improving the photocatalytic activity and photon utilization efficiency [45]. When the ratio of Ir:Pr increases further, the energy level defect of TiO2 is increased, which reduces the photocatalytic ability.

The desulfurization and denitrogenation ratio over Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) relied on the space velocity and oxidant dosage under ultraviolet radiation. The desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios decreased from 99.7 to 97.9% and from 97.1 to 95.1% when the space velocity increased from 0.1 to 1.6 h−1 (Fig. 5 a and b). Fewer light-induced holes and electrons were produced due to increasing space velocity, which reduced the photooxidation of sulfides and nitrides. However, when the space velocity was too small, the transmittance of suspension would be reduced because the photocatalyst dosage was too much. When increasing the dosage of H2O2, the increasing H2O2 and its decomposition promoted the oxidization reactions, consequently increasing the desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios. Nevertheless, when the dosage of H2O2 was high, desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios decreased, because the high •OH concentration produced by H2O2 resulted in the reverse reaction of H2O2 → •OH + •OH. The maximum of desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios appeared, where the dosage of H2O2 arrived at 2.5% and 3.0%, respectively [46].

The reusability of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was carried out under optimized conditions for the desulfurization and denitrogenation ratio of sulfides and nitrides as the model component. The catalyst was separated from the reaction system at the end of the desulfurization process by simple filtration, washed with acetone, and then dried at 373 K for 2 h. The recycled catalyst was processed for the consequent reaction under optimized conditions. After each catalytic run, the refined oil was analyzed to determine the concentration of sulfides and nitrides. The data in Fig. 6 a and 6b revealed that there is no obvious decrease in the activity of the catalyst even after eleven successive runs. Compared with Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:0.5), Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:0.8), and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1.5), the ratios of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) is no obvious decrease in the activity of the catalyst even after eleven successive runs (Fig. 6a and 6b), showing its excellent consistency. From Fig. S3(b), compared with the fresh catalyst, the recovered catalyst lattice did not change, which proves that the catalyst still has high stability after the reaction. It could be clarified by the synergistic effect between Ir/Pr and TiO2 promotes to form surface oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ defects, which can promote the separation and transport of photo-generated charge carriers. The slight decrease in the removal ratios can be referred to catalyst loss during recycling and blockage of the catalyst surface. Based on the above evidence, it can be concluded that the designed Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) is a promising heterogeneous catalyst in terms of catalytic activity and reusability.

Three catalysts are continuing to lose weight, and the TG curve slope is small showing slow weight loss in the range of reaction temperature (Fig. 6c). The weight loss of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1) is lower than that of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 (1:1.5) and Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2(1:0.8) between 300 and 575 K, showing adding a suitable Ir/Pr ratio is conducive to the stability of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 in the reaction temperature, which is consistent with the reused catalyst results (Fig. 6a and b). Based on the results of the experiment and the characterization, the mechanism of desulfurization with the simultaneous denitrogenation over Ir/Pr-N-CQDs -TiO2 under ultraviolet radiation is proposed in Scheme S1.

The co-doping of N, CQDs, Ir, and Pr broadened the TiO2 absorption region from the ultraviolet to the visible-light region. Abundant Ti3+ led to the Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy impurity level below TiO2 CB [47], so narrowing the bandgap is helpful in the absorption of ultraviolet radiation. First, electrons could be excited from CB of TiO2 to the CQDs under ultraviolet radiation [48], promoting hole-electron separation. Second, self-doped Ti3+ of mesoporous TiO2 could be excited by ultraviolet radiation to produce holes and electrons, and the electrons might be captured by the CQDs and Ir/Pr [49]. Therefore, the hole-electron separation efficiency is increased and the lifetime of the hole is improved. Holes are involved in the oxidation of sulfides and nitrides. Finally, sulfides and nitrides are oxidized into corresponding oxides. Therefore, the synergistic effect of N, CQDs, Ir, Pr, oxygen vacancies, and Ti3+ is responsible for the photocatalytic activity of Ir/Pr- N-CQDs-TiO2. CQDs deposited on TiO2 play two roles in photocatalysis (Scheme S2). First, CQDs can be as photogenerated electron transporters and collectors, therefore the holes and electrons transferred to the surface could be separated efficiently. Then the holes and electrons have time to form other radicals to oxidize nitrides and sulfides or to serve as reaction centers [50]. Second, CQDs could help absorb light for exciting TiO2, and absorb longer wavelength light in the ultraviolet radiation range [51].

In order to investigate the effect of different catalysts, a series of simultaneous desulfurization and denitrogenation reactions were carried out. The comparative results of catalytic activity of Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2, MnO2/UiO-66-NaClO, Ti3C2/g-C3N4, and PWV5/Si-TMA were listed in Table S2. The desulfurization and denitrogenation ratios of MnO2/UiO-66-NaClO were 90% and 87%, respectively. Apparently, the Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was the most effective catalyst among the reported ones [52–54].

Ir/Pr-N-CQDs-TiO2 was prepared by a facile method and characterized in detail. The obtained catalyst displayed the excellent desulfurization and denitrogenation performance under ultraviolet radiation. The desulfurization ratio of 99.8% and denitrogenation ratio of 95.5% for diesel were achieved in suitable conditions. The experimental results show the technology was a solution to meet the environmental challenges imposed by new regulations. It is possible to decrease the sulfur and nitrogen concentration of diesel to less than 1 μg/g. Besides, the effects of space velocity, reaction time and temperature, oxidant dosage, and metal loading were investigated. The co-doping of Ir, Pr, N, CQDs in TiO2 could introduce a new trapping energy level and produce more oxygen vacancies and defects, which extend the light absorption range, improve the photo-generated charge transfer, and increase the removal ratios of sulfide and nitrides.

Lei Wang: . Ning Zuo: . Meng Sun: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yingying Ma: . Nchare Mominou: Writing - review & editing. Weijie Jiang: . Shuzhen Li: . Chunyu Jing: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The work carried out at Shanghai Institute of Technology was sponsored by Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No.19ZR1454900), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118861.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
